pages.full_text:
[hand-drawn sketch of Leamaneh Castle — architectural elevation showing square tower and attached house with windows and arched doorway]

__Leamaneh Castle__    Clare.
original sq tower with 1480 house added ?

We stopped by the road next to Leamaneh Castle, + reached the pub, The Archway, at 8.45. We had good chicken for supper. The music, guitar + fiddle, started at 9.40., + we stayed for about 45 min. It was in Ennistymon, 3 miles from campsite.

__Tuesday 13th Aug.__
	We packed up + set off. Roads slow + bumpy
pages.notes: The page contains a hand-drawn architectural sketch of Leamaneh Castle (Co. Clare) showing a square tower with attached house facade — not a route map. No handwritten page number visible at top-left. The heading 'Leamaneh Castle' is underlined; 'Clare.' follows ununderlined. The text reads 'original sq tower with 1480 house added ?' — '1480' likely refers to a date of construction/extension, with '?' expressing the diarist's uncertainty. 'Tuesday 13th Aug.' — Tuesday 13 August fits 1996 in the 1989–2003 range; prior spread (A_0183) also tentatively suggests 1996; iso_guess uses 1996 with moderate confidence. Text continues onto right page (A_0187_R).

pages.full_text:
Simon went to shop in Ennis. Eve had no money to get into Ennis Friary ruins. When he came back there was just time to see the friary, built by the O'Briens, for Franciscans. Some lovely carvings. Christ looking over a parapet, with his hands joined infront of him.
	We had lunch next to a field. Eve was upset with Peter's + John's fighting. On to __Clonmacnoise__. (Gathering of the Sons of Noye.) Cieran founded it with the help of Prince Diarmid, but Cieran died 7 months later of the Plague. It was on one of the raised escarpment paths through the peat, running from Dublin to the west coast, + on the N.S. running Shannon. Good modern buildings, well set out. Frequently sacked by the Vikings + Irish : finally destroyed by the Tudors.
	We have come on to [First] __Lough Ree__ campsite [****½] near [Alt] Athlone, on a very large lake formed by the Shannon. Thomas rude at supper.
	__Wed 14th August__
	10.50. Simon, Thomas, Peter set off to do
pages.notes: No handwritten page number visible at top-right (edge may be cropped). Text continues directly from left page (A_0187_L) mid-entry. 'Ennis Friary' is an informal name for the Franciscan friary at Ennis, Co. Clare. 'Clonmacnoise' is underlined. 'Sons of Noye' — could also read 'Noye' as 'Noye' or possibly 'Noye' (Irish: Nois); transcribed as written. 'Cieran' — standard spelling is Ciarán; transcribed as diary spells it. 'N.S. running Shannon' — appears to mean the N-S (north-south) running of the River Shannon. Above 'Lough Ree campsite' the word 'First' appears as a superscript annotation (possibly indicating this is the first time they stayed here), and '****½' appears as a campsite rating annotation. 'near Alt Athlone' — 'Alt' appears as a struck-through word before 'Athlone'; transcribed with brackets. 'infront' is the diary's spelling (one word). Page ends mid-entry continuing on next spread.
